top_review: Pop has been an Oldham Street institution for years - great selection of vintage jackets downstairs plus their own recycled and reworked line.
independent_ownership: No - multi-site chain (Manchester, Liverpool, London, Leeds, Nottingham/Newcastle, Gothenburg)
research_notes: Trading in Afflecks Palace since 1984/85, since expanded to multiple UK cities plus Gothenburg, Sweden. No specific named brands surfaced in research beyond generic 'vintage'/'recycled' marketing copy, so Brand Fit is Not confirmed rather than guessed at Strong.

top_review: Huge range of vintage clothing from 90s sportswear to 70s bohemian dresses, plus regular kilo sales - quality justifies the steeper prices, reviewers say.
price_band: Kilo sales confirmed at GBP10-20/kg; individual item pricing not confirmed
independent_ownership: No - 2-store chain (Manchester + Leeds), founded in Yorkshire in 1997
research_notes: Two confirmed physical locations (Tier 1 on locations alone). No named brands surfaced in sourced reviews - stock is described generically as 'vintage', 'reworked' and 'sportswear'.

top_review: Well-curated vintage 70s and 80s menswear and old-school sportswear - reviewers name-check Adidas, Tommy Hilfiger and Burberry pieces alongside original Gore-Tex jackets.
research_notes: Brand Fit scored Medium on 3 named brands (Adidas, Tommy Hilfiger, Burberry) confirmed via customer reviews. No dedicated ecommerce site found, so ecommerce_present is False rather than assumed.

top_review: A 70s rock n roll inspired vintage store tucked above a Chinatown restaurant - vintage clothing, gifts and homewares with real character.
research_notes: Instagram follower count (2,372) directly confirmed from the account itself. Falls in the Tier 2 Instagram range (500-4,999) on that basis alone.

full_address: Unit 8, Smithfield Buildings, 51 Oldham Street, Manchester, M1 1JR
top_review: Hand-selected vintage and designer clothing, each piece carefully researched by the in-store team, as part of a new Oxfam Vintage concept format.
independent_ownership: No - charity-run (Oxfam), centrally curated, not independently owned
research_notes: Deliberate real-world test of the charity-name override: 'Oxfam' in the name triggers the same CHARITY_NAME_PATTERN check as a dummy-data charity shop. The sourced review text uses only generic 'vintage and designer' language, not a named-brand or literal 'curated vintage'/'designer vintage' phrase, so it does NOT clear the strong-evidence override and is disqualified - proving qualify.py treats a real charity-linked shop exactly like the dummy-data equivalent, per the brief.

top_review: Two floors of handpicked vintage clothing from the 60s through the 00s, ethically sourced with worldwide online shipping.
independent_ownership: No - 7-store UK chain (Birmingham, Bristol, Leeds, Liverpool, London, Manchester, Nottingham)
research_notes: 7 confirmed UK locations comfortably clears Tier 1 on locations alone. Confirmed dedicated ecommerce site (wearecow.com) with worldwide shipping. Inventory scale marked Large on the sourced description of the Manchester unit as a genuine 2-storey shop, not a guess.

full_address: Afflecks, Church Street, Manchester, M4 1PW (2nd floor)
top_review: A vintage clothing exchange - bring unwanted clothes and trade for pre-loved vintage garments at an affordable price, now with a second shop in Withington.
independent_ownership: Yes - founder-run (Erin Taylor-Thomas), independent despite operating 2 locations
research_notes: Flagship (Afflecks) plus a confirmed second Withington location clears Tier 1 on locations alone, independent of the strong Instagram following.

top_review: Racks of retro clothing spanning the 1940s to today, plus fancy dress and costume accessories for parties - an Afflecks tenant since it opened in 1982.
research_notes: Deliberately included as a real positive-and-negative-signal collision: the sourced review genuinely mentions both retro clothing AND fancy dress/costume stock. Because the positive vintage-clothing signal is present, qualify.py's logic qualifies it rather than disqualifying on the fancy-dress mention alone (positive wins when both appear together) - the same rule the dummy scrape relies on.

top_review: 1950s-focused vintage womenswear, sourced from around the world and reworked shorter to be more wearable today.
research_notes: Real-world instance of Known Pitfall #4 (never trust a field match without corroborating context): search results surfaced an Instagram account called '@retrorehab' with roughly 5,900 followers, but that account is a mid-century FURNITURE restoration business unrelated to this clothing shop - a coincidental name collision, not the same business. The clothing shop's own account could not be confirmed with a verified follower count, so ig_followers is left Not confirmed rather than misattributing the furniture account's number.

top_review: Premium hand-picked vintage and modern classic menswear since 2005 - Barbour jackets, old-school Berghaus and Rab Gore-Tex, Adidas jumpers and Levi's jeans in abundance.
research_notes: Brand Fit is genuinely Strong here (5 named brands confirmed in sourced coverage: Barbour, Berghaus, Rab, Adidas, Levi's), but the Tier 1 'Strong Brand Fit' criterion also requires confirmed active ecommerce, which research did not turn up for this shop - no dedicated online store found. Result: Tier 3 despite the strong brand signal, a real example of why Brand Fit alone doesn't guarantee a high tier, and a good candidate for a BDR to call directly and confirm ecommerce before re-scoring.

full_address: Makers Quarter, Aytoun Street, Manchester, M1 3GL
top_review: A curated vintage and designer clothing studio - quiet luxury staples, natural fibres and timeless tailoring, open for browsing by appointment and monthly open studio days.
price_band: Positioned as 'quiet luxury'; no specific item prices confirmed
research_notes: Instagram follower count (24,000) directly confirmed from the account, clearing Tier 1 on Instagram alone. 'Quiet luxury' and 'natural fibres' are styling descriptors, not named brands, so Brand Fit is Not confirmed rather than Strong - consistent with the rule that generic designer language doesn't count.
